lesson.title: NumPy Operations
lesson.body: NumPy applies arithmetic operators element-wise between arrays of the same shape — no loops needed. Broadcasting extends this to arrays of different shapes by automatically aligning dimensions: a (3,) array can broadcast against a (4, 3) array along axis 0. Indexing and slicing use arr[row, col] syntax; boolean indexing filters with a condition array. np.where(condition, x, y) returns x where condition is True and y elsewhere.

rooms.question:
What does this code print?

import numpy as np
a = np.array([1, 2, 3])
b = np.array([10, 20, 30])
print(a * b)
rooms.explanation: NumPy multiplication is element-wise: [1*10, 2*20, 3*30] = [10, 40, 90]. This is NOT a dot product.

rooms.question: Complete the code to select all elements greater than 5 from an array:
rooms.code:
import numpy as np
arr = np.array([2, 7, 1, 9, 4, 6])
result = arr[arr ___ 5]
print(result)
rooms.explanation: arr > 5 creates a boolean array [False, True, False, True, False, True]. Using it as an index selects only the True positions: [7, 9, 6].

rooms.question: Complete the code to replace all negative values with 0 using np.where:
rooms.code:
import numpy as np
arr = np.array([-1, 2, -3, 4])
result = np.___(arr >= 0, arr, 0)
print(result)
rooms.explanation: np.where(condition, x, y) returns elements from x where condition is True and from y (here 0) where it is False. This cleanly replaces negatives with 0.

rooms.question:
What does this code print?

import numpy as np
arr = np.arange(9).reshape(3, 3)
print(arr[1:3, 0:2])
Order the NumPy broadcasting rules that determine if two shapes are compatible:
items: Dimensions match if equal or one is 1
rooms.explanation: NumPy aligns shapes right-to-left, pads with 1s on the left if needed, checks each pair (must be equal or one must be 1), then stretches the size-1 dimensions to match the other.

boss.name: The Broadcasting Daemon
boss.taunt: Shapes (3, 4) and (3,) cannot broadcast! Read the rules or suffer ValueError!

questions.question: Can np.ones((3, 4)) and np.ones((4,)) be added together with broadcasting?
questions.explanation: Broadcasting aligns from the right. (4,) becomes (1, 4), which then stretches to (3, 4) to match. Each row of the (3,4) array gets the (4,) added to it.

questions.question: Complete the code to get the indices that would sort an array:
questions.code:
import numpy as np
arr = np.array([30, 10, 20])
idx = np.___(arr)
print(idx)
questions.explanation: np.argsort() returns the indices that would sort the array. For [30, 10, 20] the result is [1, 2, 0] — index 1 (value 10) comes first.

questions.question:
What does this code print?

import numpy as np
arr = np.array([[1,2,3],[4,5,6]])
print(arr.sum(axis=0))
questions.explanation: axis=0 collapses along rows (summing down each column). Column sums: 1+4=5, 2+5=7, 3+6=9. Result: [5, 7, 9].
